Go to the documentation of this file.

00029 
00030 /*
00031  * Core kernel-level thread system.
00032  */
00033 
00034 #include <types.h>
00035 #include <kern/errno.h>
00036 #include <lib.h>
00037 #include <array.h>
00038 #include <cpu.h>
00039 #include <spl.h>
00040 #include <spinlock.h>
00041 #include <wchan.h>
00042 #include <syscall.h>
00043 #include <thread.h>
00044 #include <threadlist.h>
00045 #include <threadprivate.h>
00046 #include <current.h>
00047 #include <synch.h>
00048 #include <addrspace.h>
00049 #include <mainbus.h>
00050 #include <vnode.h>
00051 #include "opt-synchprobs.h"
00052 
00053 
00054 /* Magic number used as a guard value on kernel thread stacks. */
00055 #define THREAD_STACK_MAGIC 0xbaadf00d
00056 
00057 /* used pid table */
00058 char pid_used[MAX_THREAD];
00059 
00060 /* index for the used pid table */
00061 static int pid_index;
00062 
00063 /* threads table */
00064 struct thread* thread_table[MAX_THREAD];
00065 
00066 /* Wait channel. */
00067 struct wchan {
00068         const char *wc_name;            /* name for this channel */
00069         struct threadlist wc_threads;   /* list of waiting threads */
00070         struct spinlock wc_lock;        /* lock for mutual exclusion */
00071 };
00072 
00073 /* Master array of CPUs. */
00074 DECLARRAY(cpu);
00075 DEFARRAY(cpu, /*no inline*/ );
00076 static struct cpuarray allcpus;
00077 
00078 /* Used to wait for secondary CPUs to come online. */
00079 static struct semaphore *cpu_startup_sem;
00080 
00082 
00083 /*
00084  * Stick a magic number on the bottom end of the stack. This will
00085  * (sometimes) catch kernel stack overflows. Use thread_checkstack()
00086  * to test this.
00087  */
00088 static
00089 void
00090 thread_checkstack_init(struct thread *thread)
00091 {
00092         ((uint32_t *)thread->t_stack)[0] = THREAD_STACK_MAGIC;
00093         ((uint32_t *)thread->t_stack)[1] = THREAD_STACK_MAGIC;
00094         ((uint32_t *)thread->t_stack)[2] = THREAD_STACK_MAGIC;
00095         ((uint32_t *)thread->t_stack)[3] = THREAD_STACK_MAGIC;
00096 }
00097 
00098 /*
00099  * Check the magic number we put on the bottom end of the stack in
00100  * thread_checkstack_init. If these assertions go off, it most likely
00101  * means you overflowed your stack at some point, which can cause all
00102  * kinds of mysterious other things to happen.
00103  *
00104  * Note that when ->t_stack is NULL, which is the case if the stack
00105  * cannot be freed (which in turn is the case if the stack is the boot
00106  * stack, and the thread is the boot thread) this doesn't do anything.
00107  */
00108 static
00109 void
00110 thread_checkstack(struct thread *thread)
00111 {
00112         if (thread->t_stack != NULL) {
00113                 KASSERT(((uint32_t*)thread->t_stack)[0] == THREAD_STACK_MAGIC);
00114                 KASSERT(((uint32_t*)thread->t_stack)[1] == THREAD_STACK_MAGIC);
00115                 KASSERT(((uint32_t*)thread->t_stack)[2] == THREAD_STACK_MAGIC);
00116                 KASSERT(((uint32_t*)thread->t_stack)[3] == THREAD_STACK_MAGIC);
00117         }
00118 }
00119 
00120 /*
00121  * Create a thread. This is used both to create a first thread
00122  * for each CPU and to create subsequent forked threads.
00123  */
00124 static
00125 struct thread *
00126 thread_create(const char *name)
00127 {
00128         struct thread *thread;
00129 
00130         DEBUGASSERT(name != NULL);
00131 
00132         thread = kmalloc(sizeof(*thread));
00133         if (thread == NULL) {
00134                 return NULL;
00135         }
00136 
00137         thread->t_name = kstrdup(name);
00138         if (thread->t_name == NULL) {
00139                 kfree(thread);
00140                 return NULL;
00141         }
00142         thread->t_wchan_name = "NEW";
00143         thread->t_state = S_READY;
00144 
00145         /* Thread subsystem fields */
00146         thread_machdep_init(&thread->t_machdep);
00147         threadlistnode_init(&thread->t_listnode, thread);
00148         thread->t_stack = NULL;
00149         thread->t_context = NULL;
00150         thread->t_cpu = NULL;
00151 
00152         /* Interrupt state fields */
00153         thread->t_in_interrupt = false;
00154         thread->t_curspl = IPL_HIGH;
00155         thread->t_iplhigh_count = 1; /* corresponding to t_curspl */
00156 
00157         /* VM fields */
00158         thread->t_addrspace = NULL;
00159 
00160         /* VFS fields */
00161         thread->t_cwd = NULL;
00162 
00163         /* If you add to struct thread, be sure to initialize here */
00164 
00165         /* Inizialize open files table */
00166         init_file_table(thread);
00167 
00168         /* Set standard file descriptor */
00169         if (con_attached==true) {
00170                 set_std_fd(thread);
00171         }
00172 
00173         /* Initialize thread info */
00174         thread->thread_id = 0;
00175         thread->parent_id = 0;
00176         thread->exit_code = 0;
00177 
00178         return thread;
00179 }
00180 
00181 /*
00182  * Create a CPU structure. This is used for the bootup CPU and
00183  * also for secondary CPUs.
00184  *
00185  * The hardware number (the number assigned by firmware or system
00186  * board config or whatnot) is tracked separately because it is not
00187  * necessarily anything sane or meaningful.
00188  */
00189 struct cpu *
00190 cpu_create(unsigned hardware_number)
00191 {
00192         struct cpu *c;
00193         int result;
00194         char namebuf[16];
00195 
00196         c = kmalloc(sizeof(*c));
00197         if (c == NULL) {
00198                 panic("cpu_create: Out of memory\n");
00199         }
00200         
00201         c->c_self = c;
00202         c->c_hardware_number = hardware_number;
00203 
00204         c->c_curthread = NULL;
00205         threadlist_init(&c->c_zombies);
00206         c->c_hardclocks = 0;
00207 
00208         c->c_isidle = false;
00209         threadlist_init(&c->c_runqueue);
00210         spinlock_init(&c->c_runqueue_lock);
00211 
00212         c->c_ipi_pending = 0;
00213         c->c_numshootdown = 0;
00214         spinlock_init(&c->c_ipi_lock);
00215 
00216         result = cpuarray_add(&allcpus, c, &c->c_number);
00217         if (result != 0) {
00218                 panic("cpu_create: array_add: %s\n", strerror(result));
00219         }
00220 
00221         snprintf(namebuf, sizeof(namebuf), "<boot #%d>", c->c_number);
00222         c->c_curthread = thread_create(namebuf);
00223         if (c->c_curthread == NULL) {
00224                 panic("cpu_create: thread_create failed\n");
00225         }
00226 
00227         if (c->c_number == 0) {
00228                 /*
00229                  * Leave c->c_curthread->t_stack NULL for the boot
00230                  * cpu. This means we're using the boot stack, which
00231                  * can't be freed. (Exercise: what would it take to
00232                  * make it possible to free the boot stack?)
00233                  */
00234                 /*c->c_curthread->t_stack = ... */
00235         }
00236         else {
00237                 c->c_curthread->t_stack = kmalloc(STACK_SIZE);
00238                 if (c->c_curthread->t_stack == NULL) {
00239                         panic("cpu_create: couldn't allocate stack");
00240                 }
00241                 thread_checkstack_init(c->c_curthread);
00242         }
00243         c->c_curthread->t_cpu = c;
00244 
00245         cpu_machdep_init(c);
00246 
00247         return c;
00248 }
00249 
00250 /*
00251  * Destroy a thread.
00252  *
00253  * This function cannot be called in the victim thread's own context.
00254  * Nor can it be called on a running thread.
00255  *
00256  * (Freeing the stack you're actually using to run is ... inadvisable.)
00257  */
00258 static
00259 void
00260 thread_destroy(struct thread *thread)
00261 {
00262         KASSERT(thread != curthread);
00263         KASSERT(thread->t_state != S_RUN);
00264 
00265         /*
00266          * If you add things to struct thread, be sure to clean them up
00267          * either here or in thread_exit(). (And not both...)
00268          */
00269 
00270         /* VFS fields, cleaned up in thread_exit */
00271         KASSERT(thread->t_cwd == NULL);
00272 
00273         /* VM fields, cleaned up in thread_exit */
00274         KASSERT(thread->t_addrspace == NULL);
00275 
00276         /* Thread subsystem fields */
00277         if (thread->t_stack != NULL) {
00278                 kfree(thread->t_stack);
00279         }
00280         threadlistnode_cleanup(&thread->t_listnode);
00281         thread_machdep_cleanup(&thread->t_machdep);
00282 
00283         /* sheer paranoia */
00284         thread->t_wchan_name = "DESTROYED";
00285 
00286         kfree(thread->t_name);
00287         kfree(thread);
00288 }
00289 
00290 /*
00291  * Clean up zombies. (Zombies are threads that have exited but still
00292  * need to have thread_destroy called on them.)
00293  *
00294  * The list of zombies is per-cpu.
00295  */
00296 static
00297 void
00298 exorcise(void)
00299 {
00300         struct thread *z;
00301 
00302         while ((z = threadlist_remhead(&curcpu->c_zombies)) != NULL) {
00303                 KASSERT(z != curthread);
00304                 KASSERT(z->t_state == S_ZOMBIE);
00305                 thread_destroy(z);
00306         }
00307 }
00308 
00309 /*
00310  * On panic, stop the thread system (as much as is reasonably
00311  * possible) to make sure we don't end up letting any other threads
00312  * run.
00313  */
00314 void
00315 thread_panic(void)
00316 {
00317         /*
00318          * Kill off other CPUs.
00319          *
00320          * We could wait for them to stop, except that they might not.
00321          */
00322         ipi_broadcast(IPI_PANIC);
00323 
00324         /*
00325          * Drop runnable threads on the floor.
00326          *
00327          * Don't try to get the run queue lock; we might not be able
00328          * to.  Instead, blat the list structure by hand, and take the
00329          * risk that it might not be quite atomic.
00330          */
00331         curcpu->c_runqueue.tl_count = 0;
00332         curcpu->c_runqueue.tl_head.tln_next = NULL;
00333         curcpu->c_runqueue.tl_tail.tln_prev = NULL;
00334 
00335         /*
00336          * Ideally, we want to make sure sleeping threads don't wake
00337          * up and start running. However, there's no good way to track
00338          * down all the wchans floating around the system. Another
00339          * alternative would be to set a global flag to make the wchan
00340          * wakeup operations do nothing; but that would mean we
00341          * ourselves couldn't sleep to wait for an I/O completion
00342          * interrupt, and we'd like to be able to do that if the
00343          * system isn't that badly hosed.
00344          *
00345          * So, do nothing else here.
00346          *
00347          * This may prove inadequate in practice and further steps
00348          * might be needed. It may also be necessary to go through and
00349          * forcibly unlock all locks or the like...
00350          */
00351 }
00352 
00353 /*
00354  * At system shutdown, ask the other CPUs to switch off.
00355  */
00356 void
00357 thread_shutdown(void)
00358 {
00359         /*
00360          * Stop the other CPUs.
00361          *
00362          * We should probably wait for them to stop and shut them off
00363          * on the system board.
00364          */
00365         ipi_broadcast(IPI_OFFLINE);
00366 }
00367 
00368 /*
00369  * Thread system initialization.
00370  */
00371 void
00372 thread_bootstrap(void)
00373 {
00374         struct cpu *bootcpu;
00375         struct thread *bootthread;
00376         int i;
00377 
00378         cpuarray_init(&allcpus);
00379 
00380         /*
00381          * Create the cpu structure for the bootup CPU, the one we're
00382          * currently running on. Assume the hardware number is 0; that
00383          * might be updated later by mainbus-type code. This also
00384          * creates a thread structure for the first thread, the one
00385          * that's already implicitly running when the kernel is
00386          * started from the bootloader.
00387          */
00388         bootcpu = cpu_create(0);
00389         bootthread = bootcpu->c_curthread;
00390 
00391         /*
00392          * Initializing curcpu and curthread is machine-dependent
00393          * because either of curcpu and curthread might be defined in
00394          * terms of the other.
00395          */
00396         INIT_CURCPU(bootcpu, bootthread);
00397 
00398         /*
00399          * Now make sure both t_cpu and c_curthread are set. This
00400          * might be partially redundant with INIT_CURCPU depending on
00401          * how things are defined.
00402          */
00403         curthread->t_cpu = curcpu;
00404         curcpu->c_curthread = curthread;
00405 
00406         /* Set thread id */
00407         bootthread->thread_id = 1;
00408         bootthread->parent_id = 0;
00409         pid_index = 1;
00410         pid_used[0] = 1;
00411         pid_used[1] = 1;
00412 
00413         thread_table[0] = NULL;
00414         thread_table[1] = bootthread;
00415 
00416         /* Initialize all other */
00417         for (i =2; i<MAX_THREAD; i++) {
00418                 pid_used[i] = 0;
00419                 thread_table[i] = NULL;
00420         }
00421 
00422         /* Done */
00423 }
00424 
00425 /*
00426  * New CPUs come here once MD initialization is finished. curthread
00427  * and curcpu should already be initialized.
00428  *
00429  * Other than clearing thread_start_cpus() to continue, we don't need
00430  * to do anything. The startup thread can just exit; we only need it
00431  * to be able to get into thread_switch() properly.
00432  */
00433 void
00434 cpu_hatch(unsigned software_number)
00435 {
00436         KASSERT(curcpu != NULL);
00437         KASSERT(curthread != NULL);
00438         KASSERT(curcpu->c_number == software_number);
00439 
00440         spl0();
00441 
00442         kprintf("cpu%u: %s\n", software_number, cpu_identify());
00443 
00444         V(cpu_startup_sem);
00445         thread_exit();
00446 }
00447 
00448 /*
00449  * Start up secondary cpus. Called from boot().
00450  */
00451 void
00452 thread_start_cpus(void)
00453 {
00454         unsigned i;
00455 
00456         kprintf("cpu0: %s\n", cpu_identify());
00457 
00458         cpu_startup_sem = sem_create("cpu_hatch", 0);
00459         mainbus_start_cpus();
00460         
00461         for (i=0; i<cpuarray_num(&allcpus) - 1; i++) {
00462                 P(cpu_startup_sem);
00463         }
00464         sem_destroy(cpu_startup_sem);
00465         cpu_startup_sem = NULL;
00466 }
00467 
00468 /*
00469  * Make a thread runnable.
00470  *
00471  * targetcpu might be curcpu; it might not be, too. 
00472  */
00473 static
00474 void
00475 thread_make_runnable(struct thread *target, bool already_have_lock)
00476 {
00477         struct cpu *targetcpu;
00478         bool isidle;
00479 
00480         /* Lock the run queue of the target thread's cpu. */
00481         targetcpu = target->t_cpu;
00482 
00483         if (already_have_lock) {
00484                 /* The target thread's cpu should be already locked. */
00485                 KASSERT(spinlock_do_i_hold(&targetcpu->c_runqueue_lock));
00486         }
00487         else {
00488                 spinlock_acquire(&targetcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00489         }
00490 
00491         isidle = targetcpu->c_isidle;
00492         threadlist_addtail(&targetcpu->c_runqueue, target);
00493         if (isidle) {
00494                 /*
00495                  * Other processor is idle; send interrupt to make
00496                  * sure it unidles.
00497                  */
00498                 ipi_send(targetcpu, IPI_UNIDLE);
00499         }
00500 
00501         if (!already_have_lock) {
00502                 spinlock_release(&targetcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00503         }
00504 }
00505 
00506 /*
00507  * Create a new thread based on an existing one.
00508  *
00509  * The new thread has name NAME, and starts executing in function
00510  * ENTRYPOINT. DATA1 and DATA2 are passed to ENTRYPOINT.
00511  *
00512  * The new thread is given no address space (the caller decides that)
00513  * but inherits its current working directory from the caller. It will
00514  * start on the same CPU as the caller, unless the scheduler
00515  * intervenes first.
00516  */
00517 int
00518 thread_fork(const char *name,
00519             void (*entrypoint)(void *data1, unsigned long data2),
00520             void *data1, unsigned long data2,
00521             struct thread **ret)
00522 {
00523         struct thread *newthread;
00524         int temp;
00525 
00526         newthread = thread_create(name);
00527         if (newthread == NULL) {
00528                 return ENOMEM;
00529         }
00530 
00531         /* Allocate a stack */
00532         newthread->t_stack = kmalloc(STACK_SIZE);
00533         if (newthread->t_stack == NULL) {
00534                 thread_destroy(newthread);
00535                 return ENOMEM;
00536         }
00537         thread_checkstack_init(newthread);
00538 
00539         /* Find next pid */
00540         temp = find_nextpid();
00541 
00542         /* If there is not free pid, free the thread */
00543         if (temp < 0) {
00544                 if (newthread->t_cwd != NULL) {
00545                         VOP_DECREF(newthread->t_cwd);
00546                 }
00547                 kfree(newthread->t_stack);
00548                 kfree(newthread->t_name);
00549                 kfree(newthread);
00550 
00551                 return EAGAIN;
00552         }
00553         newthread->thread_id = pid_index;
00554         newthread->parent_id = curthread->thread_id;
00555 
00556         /* Set the pid as used */
00557         pid_used[pid_index] = 1;
00558 
00559         /* Put the thread in the thread table */
00560         thread_table[pid_index] = newthread;
00561 
00562         /*
00563          * Now we clone various fields from the parent thread.
00564          */
00565 
00566         /* Thread subsystem fields */
00567         newthread->t_cpu = curthread->t_cpu;
00568 
00569         /* VM fields */
00570         /* do not clone address space -- let caller decide on that */
00571 
00572         /* VFS fields */
00573         if (curthread->t_cwd != NULL) {
00574                 VOP_INCREF(curthread->t_cwd);
00575                 newthread->t_cwd = curthread->t_cwd;
00576         }
00577 
00578         /*
00579          * Because new threads come out holding the cpu runqueue lock
00580          * (see notes at bottom of thread_switch), we need to account
00581          * for the spllower() that will be done releasing it.
00582          */
00583         newthread->t_iplhigh_count++;
00584 
00585         /* Set up the switchframe so entrypoint() gets called */
00586         switchframe_init(newthread, entrypoint, data1, data2);
00587 
00588         /* Lock the current cpu's run queue and make the new thread runnable */
00589         thread_make_runnable(newthread, false);
00590 
00591         /*
00592          * Return new thread structure if it's wanted. Note that using
00593          * the thread structure from the parent thread should be done
00594          * only with caution, because in general the child thread
00595          * might exit at any time.
00596          */
00597         if (ret != NULL) {
00598                 *ret = newthread;
00599         }
00600 
00601         return 0;
00602 }
00603 
00604 /*
00605  * High level, machine-independent context switch code.
00606  *
00607  * The current thread is queued appropriately and its state is changed
00608  * to NEWSTATE; another thread to run is selected and switched to.
00609  *
00610  * If NEWSTATE is S_SLEEP, the thread is queued on the wait channel
00611  * WC. Otherwise WC should be NULL.
00612  */
00613 static
00614 void
00615 thread_switch(threadstate_t newstate, struct wchan *wc)
00616 {
00617         struct thread *cur, *next;
00618         int spl;
00619 
00620         DEBUGASSERT(curcpu->c_curthread == curthread);
00621         DEBUGASSERT(curthread->t_cpu == curcpu->c_self);
00622 
00623         /* Explicitly disable interrupts on this processor */
00624         spl = splhigh();
00625 
00626         cur = curthread;
00627 
00628         /*
00629          * If we're idle, return without doing anything. This happens
00630          * when the timer interrupt interrupts the idle loop.
00631          */
00632         if (curcpu->c_isidle) {
00633                 splx(spl);
00634                 return;
00635         }
00636 
00637         /* Check the stack guard band. */
00638         thread_checkstack(cur);
00639 
00640         /* Lock the run queue. */
00641         spinlock_acquire(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00642 
00643         /* Micro-optimization: if nothing to do, just return */
00644         if (newstate == S_READY && threadlist_isempty(&curcpu->c_runqueue)) {
00645                 spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00646                 splx(spl);
00647                 return;
00648         }
00649 
00650         /* Put the thread in the right place. */
00651         switch (newstate) {
00652             case S_RUN:
00653                 panic("Illegal S_RUN in thread_switch\n");
00654             case S_READY:
00655                 thread_make_runnable(cur, true /*have lock*/);
00656                 break;
00657             case S_SLEEP:
00658                 cur->t_wchan_name = wc->wc_name;
00659                 /*
00660                  * Add the thread to the list in the wait channel, and
00661                  * unlock same. To avoid a race with someone else
00662                  * calling wchan_wake*, we must keep the wchan locked
00663                  * from the point the caller of wchan_sleep locked it
00664                  * until the thread is on the list.
00665                  *
00666                  * (We could for symmetry relock the channel before
00667                  * returning from wchan_sleep, but we don't, for two
00668                  * reasons. One is that the caller is unlikely to need
00669                  * or want it locked and if it does can lock it itself
00670                  * without racing. Exercise: what's the other?)
00671                  */
00672                 threadlist_addtail(&wc->wc_threads, cur);
00673                 wchan_unlock(wc);
00674                 break;
00675             case S_ZOMBIE:
00676                 cur->t_wchan_name = "ZOMBIE";
00677                 threadlist_addtail(&curcpu->c_zombies, cur);
00678                 break;
00679         }
00680         cur->t_state = newstate;
00681 
00682         /*
00683          * Get the next thread. While there isn't one, call md_idle().
00684          * curcpu->c_isidle must be true when md_idle is
00685          * called. Unlock the runqueue while idling too, to make sure
00686          * things can be added to it.
00687          *
00688          * Note that we don't need to unlock the runqueue atomically
00689          * with idling; becoming unidle requires receiving an
00690          * interrupt (either a hardware interrupt or an interprocessor
00691          * interrupt from another cpu posting a wakeup) and idling
00692          * *is* atomic with respect to re-enabling interrupts.
00693          *
00694          * Note that c_isidle becomes true briefly even if we don't go
00695          * idle. However, because one is supposed to hold the runqueue
00696          * lock to look at it, this should not be visible or matter.
00697          */
00698 
00699         /* The current cpu is now idle. */
00700         curcpu->c_isidle = true;
00701         do {
00702                 next = threadlist_remhead(&curcpu->c_runqueue);
00703                 if (next == NULL) {
00704                         spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00705                         cpu_idle();
00706                         spinlock_acquire(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00707                 }
00708         } while (next == NULL);
00709         curcpu->c_isidle = false;
00710 
00711         /*
00712          * Note that curcpu->c_curthread may be the same variable as
00713          * curthread and it may not be, depending on how curthread and
00714          * curcpu are defined by the MD code. We'll assign both and
00715          * assume the compiler will optimize one away if they're the
00716          * same.
00717          */
00718         curcpu->c_curthread = next;
00719         curthread = next;
00720 
00721         /* do the switch (in assembler in switch.S) */
00722         switchframe_switch(&cur->t_context, &next->t_context);
00723 
00724         /*
00725          * When we get to this point we are either running in the next
00726          * thread, or have come back to the same thread again,
00727          * depending on how you look at it. That is,
00728          * switchframe_switch returns immediately in another thread
00729          * context, which in general will be executing here with a
00730          * different stack and different values in the local
00731          * variables. (Although new threads go to thread_startup
00732          * instead.) But, later on when the processor, or some
00733          * processor, comes back to the previous thread, it's also
00734          * executing here with the *same* value in the local
00735          * variables.
00736          *
00737          * The upshot, however, is as follows:
00738          *
00739          *    - The thread now currently running is "cur", not "next",
00740          *      because when we return from switchrame_switch on the
00741          *      same stack, we're back to the thread that
00742          *      switchframe_switch call switched away from, which is
00743          *      "cur".
00744          *
00745          *    - "cur" is _not_ the thread that just *called*
00746          *      switchframe_switch.
00747          *
00748          *    - If newstate is S_ZOMB we never get back here in that
00749          *      context at all.
00750          *
00751          *    - If the thread just chosen to run ("next") was a new
00752          *      thread, we don't get to this code again until
00753          *      *another* context switch happens, because when new
00754          *      threads return from switchframe_switch they teleport
00755          *      to thread_startup.
00756          *
00757          *    - At this point the thread whose stack we're now on may
00758          *      have been migrated to another cpu since it last ran.
00759          *
00760          * The above is inherently confusing and will probably take a
00761          * while to get used to.
00762          *
00763          * However, the important part is that code placed here, after
00764          * the call to switchframe_switch, does not necessarily run on
00765          * every context switch. Thus any such code must be either
00766          * skippable on some switches or also called from
00767          * thread_startup.
00768          */
00769 
00770 
00771         /* Clear the wait channel and set the thread state. */
00772         cur->t_wchan_name = NULL;
00773         cur->t_state = S_RUN;
00774 
00775         /* Unlock the run queue. */
00776         spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00777 
00778         /* If we have an address space, activate it in the MMU. */
00779         if (cur->t_addrspace != NULL) {
00780                 as_activate(cur->t_addrspace);
00781         }
00782 
00783         /* Clean up dead threads. */
00784         exorcise();
00785 
00786         /* Turn interrupts back on. */
00787         splx(spl);
00788 }
00789 
00790 /*
00791  * This function is where new threads start running. The arguments
00792  * ENTRYPOINT, DATA1, and DATA2 are passed through from thread_fork.
00793  *
00794  * Because new code comes here from inside the middle of
00795  * thread_switch, the beginning part of this function must match the
00796  * tail of thread_switch.
00797  */
00798 void
00799 thread_startup(void (*entrypoint)(void *data1, unsigned long data2),
00800                void *data1, unsigned long data2)
00801 {
00802         struct thread *cur;
00803 
00804         cur = curthread;
00805 
00806         /* Clear the wait channel and set the thread state. */
00807         cur->t_wchan_name = NULL;
00808         cur->t_state = S_RUN;
00809 
00810         /* Release the runqueue lock acquired in thread_switch. */
00811         spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00812 
00813         /* If we have an address space, activate it in the MMU. */
00814         if (cur->t_addrspace != NULL) {
00815                 as_activate(cur->t_addrspace);
00816         }
00817 
00818         /* Clean up dead threads. */
00819         exorcise();
00820 
00821         /* Enable interrupts. */
00822         spl0();
00823 
00824 #if OPT_SYNCHPROBS
00825         /* Yield a random number of times to get a good mix of threads. */
00826         {
00827                 int i, n;
00828                 n = random()%161 + random()%161;
00829                 for (i=0; i<n; i++) {
00830                         thread_yield();
00831                 }
00832         }
00833 #endif
00834 
00835         /* Call the function. */
00836         entrypoint(data1, data2);
00837 
00838         /* Done. */
00839         thread_exit();
00840 }
00841 
00842 /*
00843  * Cause the current thread to exit.
00844  *
00845  * The parts of the thread structure we don't actually need to run
00846  * should be cleaned up right away. The rest has to wait until
00847  * thread_destroy is called from exorcise().
00848  *
00849  * Does not return.
00850  */
00851 void
00852 thread_exit(void)
00853 {
00854         struct thread *cur;
00855         int i;
00856 
00857         cur = curthread;
00858 
00859         /* VFS fields */
00860         if (cur->t_cwd) {
00861                 VOP_DECREF(cur->t_cwd);
00862                 cur->t_cwd = NULL;
00863         }
00864 
00865         /* VM fields */
00866         if (cur->t_addrspace) {
00867                 /*
00868                  * Clear t_addrspace before calling as_destroy. Otherwise
00869                  * if as_destroy sleeps (which is quite possible) when we
00870                  * come back we'll call as_activate on a half-destroyed
00871                  * address space, which is usually messily fatal.
00872                  */
00873                 struct addrspace *as = cur->t_addrspace;
00874                 cur->t_addrspace = NULL;
00875                 as_activate(NULL);
00876                 as_destroy(as);
00877         }
00878 
00879         /* Check the stack guard band. */
00880         thread_checkstack(cur);
00881 
00882         /* Interrupts off on this processor */
00883         splhigh();
00884         thread_switch(S_ZOMBIE, NULL);
00885         panic("The zombie walks!\n");
00886 
00887         /* Free files memory structures */
00888         for (i=0; i<MAX_FD; i++) {
00889                 if (cur->table_fd[i]!=NULL){
00890                         kfree(cur->table_fd[i]);
00891                         --cur->last_fd;
00892                 }
00893         }
00894 }
00895 
00896 /*
00897  * Yield the cpu to another process, but stay runnable.
00898  */
00899 void
00900 thread_yield(void)
00901 {
00902         thread_switch(S_READY, NULL);
00903 }
00904 
00906 
00907 /*
00908  * Scheduler.
00909  *
00910  * This is called periodically from hardclock(). It should reshuffle
00911  * the current CPU's run queue by job priority.
00912  */
00913 
00914 void
00915 schedule(void)
00916 {
00917         /*
00918          * You can write this. If we do nothing, threads will run in
00919          * round-robin fashion.
00920          */
00921 }
00922 
00923 /*
00924  * Thread migration.
00925  *
00926  * This is also called periodically from hardclock(). If the current
00927  * CPU is busy and other CPUs are idle, or less busy, it should move
00928  * threads across to those other other CPUs.
00929  *
00930  * Migrating threads isn't free because of cache affinity; a thread's
00931  * working cache set will end up having to be moved to the other CPU,
00932  * which is fairly slow. The tradeoff between this performance loss
00933  * and the performance loss due to underutilization of some CPUs is
00934  * something that needs to be tuned and probably is workload-specific.
00935  *
00936  * For here and now, because we know we're running on System/161 and
00937  * System/161 does not (yet) model such cache effects, we'll be very
00938  * aggressive.
00939  */
00940 void
00941 thread_consider_migration(void)
00942 {
00943         unsigned my_count, total_count, one_share, to_send;
00944         unsigned i, numcpus;
00945         struct cpu *c;
00946         struct threadlist victims;
00947         struct thread *t;
00948 
00949         my_count = total_count = 0;
00950         numcpus = cpuarray_num(&allcpus);
00951         for (i=0; i<numcpus; i++) {
00952                 c = cpuarray_get(&allcpus, i);
00953                 spinlock_acquire(&c->c_runqueue_lock);
00954                 total_count += c->c_runqueue.tl_count;
00955                 if (c == curcpu->c_self) {
00956                         my_count = c->c_runqueue.tl_count;
00957                 }
00958                 spinlock_release(&c->c_runqueue_lock);
00959         }
00960 
00961         one_share = DIVROUNDUP(total_count, numcpus);
00962         if (my_count < one_share) {
00963                 return;
00964         }
00965 
00966         to_send = my_count - one_share;
00967         threadlist_init(&victims);
00968         spinlock_acquire(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00969         for (i=0; i<to_send; i++) {
00970                 t = threadlist_remtail(&curcpu->c_runqueue);
00971                 threadlist_addhead(&victims, t);
00972         }
00973         spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
00974 
00975         for (i=0; i < numcpus && to_send > 0; i++) {
00976                 c = cpuarray_get(&allcpus, i);
00977                 if (c == curcpu->c_self) {
00978                         continue;
00979                 }
00980                 spinlock_acquire(&c->c_runqueue_lock);
00981                 while (c->c_runqueue.tl_count < one_share && to_send > 0) {
00982                         t = threadlist_remhead(&victims);
00983                         /*
00984                          * Ordinarily, curthread will not appear on
00985                          * the run queue. However, it can under the
00986                          * following circumstances:
00987                          *   - it went to sleep;
00988                          *   - the processor became idle, so it
00989                          *     remained curthread;
00990                          *   - it was reawakened, so it was put on the
00991                          *     run queue;
00992                          *   - and the processor hasn't fully unidled
00993                          *     yet, so all these things are still true.
00994                          *
00995                          * If the timer interrupt happens at (almost)
00996                          * exactly the proper moment, we can come here
00997                          * while things are in this state and see
00998                          * curthread. However, *migrating* curthread
00999                          * can cause bad things to happen (Exercise:
01000                          * Why? And what?) so shuffle it to the end of
01001                          * the list and decrement to_send in order to
01002                          * skip it. Then it goes back on our own run
01003                          * queue below.
01004                          */
01005                         if (t == curthread) {
01006                                 threadlist_addtail(&victims, t);
01007                                 to_send--;
01008                                 continue;
01009                         }
01010 
01011                         t->t_cpu = c;
01012                         threadlist_addtail(&c->c_runqueue, t);
01013                         DEBUG(DB_THREADS,
01014                               "Migrated thread %s: cpu %u -> %u",
01015                               t->t_name, curcpu->c_number, c->c_number);
01016                         to_send--;
01017                         if (c->c_isidle) {
01018                                 /*
01019                                  * Other processor is idle; send
01020                                  * interrupt to make sure it unidles.
01021                                  */
01022                                 ipi_send(c, IPI_UNIDLE);
01023                         }
01024                 }
01025                 spinlock_release(&c->c_runqueue_lock);
01026         }
01027 
01028         /*
01029          * Because the code above isn't atomic, the thread counts may have
01030          * changed while we were working and we may end up with leftovers.
01031          * Don't panic; just put them back on our own run queue.
01032          */
01033         if (!threadlist_isempty(&victims)) {
01034                 spinlock_acquire(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
01035                 while ((t = threadlist_remhead(&victims)) != NULL) {
01036                         threadlist_addtail(&curcpu->c_runqueue, t);
01037                 }
01038                 spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
01039         }
01040 
01041         KASSERT(threadlist_isempty(&victims));
01042         threadlist_cleanup(&victims);
01043 }
01044 
01046 
01047 /*
01048  * Wait channel functions
01049  */
01050 
01051 /*
01052  * Create a wait channel. NAME is a symbolic string name for it.
01053  * This is what's displayed by ps -alx in Unix.
01054  *
01055  * NAME should generally be a string constant. If it isn't, alternate
01056  * arrangements should be made to free it after the wait channel is
01057  * destroyed.
01058  */
01059 struct wchan *
01060 wchan_create(const char *name)
01061 {
01062         struct wchan *wc;
01063 
01064         wc = kmalloc(sizeof(*wc));
01065         if (wc == NULL) {
01066                 return NULL;
01067         }
01068         spinlock_init(&wc->wc_lock);
01069         threadlist_init(&wc->wc_threads);
01070         wc->wc_name = name;
01071         return wc;
01072 }
01073 
01074 /*
01075  * Destroy a wait channel. Must be empty and unlocked.
01076  * (The corresponding cleanup functions require this.)
01077  */
01078 void
01079 wchan_destroy(struct wchan *wc)
01080 {
01081         spinlock_cleanup(&wc->wc_lock);
01082         threadlist_cleanup(&wc->wc_threads);
01083         kfree(wc);
01084 }
01085 
01086 /*
01087  * Lock and unlock a wait channel, respectively.
01088  */
01089 void
01090 wchan_lock(struct wchan *wc)
01091 {
01092         spinlock_acquire(&wc->wc_lock);
01093 }
01094 
01095 void
01096 wchan_unlock(struct wchan *wc)
01097 {
01098         spinlock_release(&wc->wc_lock);
01099 }
01100 
01101 /*
01102  * Yield the cpu to another process, and go to sleep, on the specified
01103  * wait channel WC. Calling wakeup on the channel will make the thread
01104  * runnable again. The channel must be locked, and will be *unlocked*
01105  * upon return.
01106  */
01107 void
01108 wchan_sleep(struct wchan *wc)
01109 {
01110         /* may not sleep in an interrupt handler */
01111         KASSERT(!curthread->t_in_interrupt);
01112 
01113         thread_switch(S_SLEEP, wc);
01114 }
01115 
01116 /*
01117  * Wake up one thread sleeping on a wait channel.
01118  */
01119 void
01120 wchan_wakeone(struct wchan *wc)
01121 {
01122         struct thread *target;
01123 
01124         /* Lock the channel and grab a thread from it */
01125         spinlock_acquire(&wc->wc_lock);
01126         target = threadlist_remhead(&wc->wc_threads);
01127         /*
01128          * Nobody else can wake up this thread now, so we don't need
01129          * to hang onto the lock.
01130          */
01131         spinlock_release(&wc->wc_lock);
01132 
01133         if (target == NULL) {
01134                 /* Nobody was sleeping. */
01135                 return;
01136         }
01137 
01138         thread_make_runnable(target, false);
01139 }
01140 
01141 /*
01142  * Wake up all threads sleeping on a wait channel.
01143  */
01144 void
01145 wchan_wakeall(struct wchan *wc)
01146 {
01147         struct thread *target;
01148         struct threadlist list;
01149 
01150         threadlist_init(&list);
01151 
01152         /*
01153          * Lock the channel and grab all the threads, moving them to a
01154          * private list.
01155          */
01156         spinlock_acquire(&wc->wc_lock);
01157         while ((target = threadlist_remhead(&wc->wc_threads)) != NULL) {
01158                 threadlist_addtail(&list, target);
01159         }
01160         /*
01161          * Nobody else can wake up these threads now, so we don't need
01162          * to hang onto the lock.
01163          */
01164         spinlock_release(&wc->wc_lock);
01165 
01166         /*
01167          * We could conceivably sort by cpu first to cause fewer lock
01168          * ops and fewer IPIs, but for now at least don't bother. Just
01169          * make each thread runnable.
01170          */
01171         while ((target = threadlist_remhead(&list)) != NULL) {
01172                 thread_make_runnable(target, false);
01173         }
01174 
01175         threadlist_cleanup(&list);
01176 }
01177 
01178 /*
01179  * Return nonzero if there are no threads sleeping on the channel.
01180  * This is meant to be used only for diagnostic purposes.
01181  */
01182 bool
01183 wchan_isempty(struct wchan *wc)
01184 {
01185         bool ret;
01186 
01187         spinlock_acquire(&wc->wc_lock);
01188         ret = threadlist_isempty(&wc->wc_threads);
01189         spinlock_release(&wc->wc_lock);
01190 
01191         return ret;
01192 }
01193 
01195 
01196 /*
01197  * Machine-independent IPI handling
01198  */
01199 
01200 /*
01201  * Send an IPI (inter-processor interrupt) to the specified CPU.
01202  */
01203 void
01204 ipi_send(struct cpu *target, int code)
01205 {
01206         KASSERT(code >= 0 && code < 32);
01207 
01208         spinlock_acquire(&target->c_ipi_lock);
01209         target->c_ipi_pending |= (uint32_t)1 << code;
01210         mainbus_send_ipi(target);
01211         spinlock_release(&target->c_ipi_lock);
01212 }
01213 
01214 void
01215 ipi_broadcast(int code)
01216 {
01217         unsigned i;
01218         struct cpu *c;
01219 
01220         for (i=0; i < cpuarray_num(&allcpus); i++) {
01221                 c = cpuarray_get(&allcpus, i);
01222                 if (c != curcpu->c_self) {
01223                         ipi_send(c, code);
01224                 }
01225         }
01226 }
01227 
01228 void
01229 ipi_tlbshootdown(struct cpu *target, const struct tlbshootdown *mapping)
01230 {
01231         int n;
01232 
01233         spinlock_acquire(&target->c_ipi_lock);
01234 
01235         n = target->c_numshootdown;
01236         if (n == TLBSHOOTDOWN_MAX) {
01237                 target->c_numshootdown = TLBSHOOTDOWN_ALL;
01238         }
01239         else {
01240                 target->c_shootdown[n] = *mapping;
01241                 target->c_numshootdown = n+1;
01242         }
01243 
01244         target->c_ipi_pending |= (uint32_t)1 << IPI_TLBSHOOTDOWN;
01245         mainbus_send_ipi(target);
01246 
01247         spinlock_release(&target->c_ipi_lock);
01248 }
01249 
01250 void
01251 interprocessor_interrupt(void)
01252 {
01253         uint32_t bits;
01254         int i;
01255 
01256         spinlock_acquire(&curcpu->c_ipi_lock);
01257         bits = curcpu->c_ipi_pending;
01258 
01259         if (bits & (1U << IPI_PANIC)) {
01260                 /* panic on another cpu - just stop dead */
01261                 cpu_halt();
01262         }
01263         if (bits & (1U << IPI_OFFLINE)) {
01264                 /* offline request */
01265                 spinlock_acquire(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
01266                 if (!curcpu->c_isidle) {
01267                         kprintf("cpu%d: offline: warning: not idle\n",
01268                                 curcpu->c_number);
01269                 }
01270                 spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_runqueue_lock);
01271                 kprintf("cpu%d: offline.\n", curcpu->c_number);
01272                 cpu_halt();
01273         }
01274         if (bits & (1U << IPI_UNIDLE)) {
01275                 /*
01276                  * The cpu has already unidled itself to take the
01277                  * interrupt; don't need to do anything else.
01278                  */
01279         }
01280         if (bits & (1U << IPI_TLBSHOOTDOWN)) {
01281                 if (curcpu->c_numshootdown == TLBSHOOTDOWN_ALL) {
01282                         vm_tlbshootdown_all();
01283                 }
01284                 else {
01285                         for (i=0; i<curcpu->c_numshootdown; i++) {
01286                                 vm_tlbshootdown(&curcpu->c_shootdown[i]);
01287                         }
01288                 }
01289                 curcpu->c_numshootdown = 0;
01290         }
01291 
01292         curcpu->c_ipi_pending = 0;
01293         spinlock_release(&curcpu->c_ipi_lock);
01294 }
01295 
01296 
01297 
01298 pid_t thread_fork2(struct trapframe* tf, int *retval)
01299 {
01300         struct thread *child;
01301         
01302         struct trapframe* newtf;
01303         
01304         int s; 
01305         int result;
01306         int tmp, i,errno;
01307         
01308         
01309 
01310         /* Check we can create a new process in the system */
01311         tmp = find_nextpid();
01312         if (tmp < 0){
01313                 errno = EMPROC;
01314                 return errno;
01315         }
01316                 
01317 
01318         /* Allocate child's thread */
01319         child = thread_create((const char *)curthread->t_name);
01320         if (child == NULL) {
01321                 errno = ENOMEM;
01322                 return errno;
01323         }
01324 
01325 
01326         
01327         /* Allocate and copy a stack    */
01328         child->t_stack = kmalloc(STACK_SIZE);
01329         if (child->t_stack==NULL) {
01330                 kfree(child->t_name);
01331                 kfree(child);
01332                 errno = ENOMEM;
01333                 return errno;
01334         }
01335         thread_checkstack_init(child);
01336 
01337 
01338         /* Copy the parent's kernel stack to the child's kernel stack */
01339         memmove((void *)child->t_stack, (const void *)curthread->t_stack, STACK_SIZE);
01340         
01341         /* Point to the trap frame of child. It is included in kernel stack */
01342         newtf = (struct trapframe *)(child->t_stack + 16);
01343 
01344         /* VFS fields, inherit the current directory */
01345         if (curthread->t_cwd != NULL) {
01346                 VOP_INCREF(curthread->t_cwd);
01347                 child->t_cwd = curthread->t_cwd;
01348         }
01349 
01350         /* Copy VM space */
01351         result = as_copy(curthread->t_addrspace, &(child->t_addrspace));
01352         if (result) {
01353                 if (child->t_cwd != NULL) {
01354                         VOP_DECREF(child->t_cwd);
01355                 }
01356                 kfree(child->t_stack);
01357                 kfree(child->t_name);
01358                 kfree(child);
01359                 return result;
01360         }
01361 
01362 
01363 
01364 
01365         child->thread_id = pid_index;
01366         child->parent_id = curthread->thread_id;
01367         
01368         /* Set the pid as used */
01369         pid_used[pid_index] = 1;
01370 
01371         /* Put thread in the thread table */
01372         thread_table[pid_index] = child;
01373 
01374         /* Thread subsystem fields */
01375         child->t_cpu=curthread->t_cpu;
01376 
01377 
01378         /* copy File System (Share file tables) */
01379         for (i = 3; i < MAX_FD; i++) {
01380                 child->table_fd[i] = curthread->table_fd[i];
01381                 if (child->table_fd[i] != NULL)
01382                         child->last_fd++;
01383         }
01384 
01385         child->t_iplhigh_count++;
01386         
01387         
01388         memmove((void *)newtf, (const void *)tf, sizeof(struct trapframe));
01389         
01390         /* Set up the switchframe so entrypoint() gets called */        
01391         switchframe_init(child,(void *)enter_forked_process,(void *) newtf, 0);
01392         
01393         /* Interrupts off for atomicity */
01394         s = splhigh();
01395 
01396         /* Make the new thread runnable */
01397         thread_make_runnable(child,false);
01398 
01399         /* Done with stuff that needs to be atomic */
01400         s=splx(s);
01401         
01402         if (child == curthread) {
01403                 *retval = 0;
01404                 
01405         } else {
01406                 
01407                 *retval = child->thread_id;
01408         }
01409         
01410 
01411         return 0;
01412 }
01413 
01414 /* Set pid_index with the next useful pid */
01415 int find_nextpid(void)
01416 {
01417         int     i, cur;
01418 
01419         cur = pid_index;
01420 
01421         for (i = cur + 1; i < MAX_THREAD; i++) {
01422         if (pid_used[i] == 0)
01423                         break;
01424         }
01425 
01426         if (i == MAX_THREAD) {
01427                 for (i = 2; i < cur; i++) {
01428                         if (pid_used[i] == 0)
01429                                 break;
01430                 }
01431 
01432                 if (i == cur)
01433                         return -1;
01434         }
01435 
01436         pid_index = i;
01437         return cur;
01438 }
01439
Generated on Sun Jun 27 22:12:13 2010 for Os/161 by  1.6.1
